questions.question: Explain the Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm with its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: KMP is an efficient string matching algorithm finding pattern in text
Time complexity: O(n + m) where n = text length, m = pattern length
Space complexity: O(m) for storing LPS array
Uses preprocessing to avoid redundant comparisons
Never backtracks in the text

questions.question: What is the LPS (Longest Proper Prefix which is also Suffix) array? How is it computed?
keyPoints: LPS array stores length of longest proper prefix that is also suffix for each position
Proper prefix = prefix that is not equal to the entire string
Computation uses dynamic approach: if characters match, extend; else fallback to previous LPS value
For first position, LPS[0] = 0
Time complexity to compute LPS is O(m)

questions.question: Differentiate between Naive string matching and KMP algorithm.
keyPoints: Naive has O(nm) worst case time complexity; KMP has O(n+m)
Naive backtracks in text after mismatch; KMP never backtracks in text
KMP uses preprocessing phase; Naive has no preprocessing
For patterns with repeated characters, naive performs much worse
KMP guarantees at most 2n comparisons during search phase

questions.question: Describe the two phases of KMP algorithm.
keyPoints: Phase 1: Preprocessing - Build LPS array from the pattern
Phase 2: Searching - Scan text using LPS to find all occurrences
Preprocessing takes O(m) time and O(m) space
Searching takes O(n) time with no backtracking in text
Both phases work together to achieve efficient matching

questions.question: What is the worst-case scenario for KMP algorithm? How does it handle overlapping matches?
keyPoints: Worst case time remains O(n+m) even with overlapping matches
When pattern has repeated characters (e.g., 'AAAA'), many overlaps can occur
KMP handles overlaps efficiently using LPS values to continue search
After finding a match, use LPS[m-1] to find next possible match
The algorithm never misses any matches, overlapping or otherwise

questions.question: Trace through the KMP algorithm to find all occurrences of pattern 'ABABC' in text 'ABABABCABABCABABC'. Show the LPS array construction and searching process.
keyPoints: First compute LPS for pattern 'ABABC': [0,0,1,2,0]
Preprocessing phase: O(m) = O(5)
Searching: Start with i=0 (text), j=0 (pattern)
Show step-by-step comparisons and matches at indices 0, 5, 10
When mismatch occurs, use LPS[j-1] to skip characters
Total comparisons = n + m = 18 + 5 = 23, less than naive approach
Three occurrences found at positions 0, 5, and 10

questions.question: Write and explain the algorithm to compute the LPS array. Analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Initialize LPS[0] = 0, len = 0, i = 1
For i from 1 to m-1: if pattern[i] == pattern[len], increment len and set LPS[i] = len
Else if len != 0: set len = LPS[len-1] and repeat comparison
Else: set LPS[i] = 0 and increment i
Time complexity: O(m) - each character processed at most twice
Space complexity: O(m) for the LPS array
Uses the property that if pattern[i] doesn't match, we can use previous LPS value

questions.question: Explain with an example why KMP is more efficient than the naive approach. Also mention real-world applications of KMP.
keyPoints: Example: pattern 'AAAA' in text 'AAAAAAAA' - naive makes O(nm)=32 comparisons, KMP makes O(n+m)=12
Naive restarts pattern from beginning after each mismatch; KMP uses LPS to skip
KMP never re-examines text characters - key optimization
Applications: Text editors (find/replace), DNA sequence matching in bioinformatics
Network intrusion detection, plagiarism detection, file comparison tools (diff)
Used in many programming languages' string searching functions

questions.question: Trace the KMP algorithm step-by-step to find pattern 'AAB' in text 'AABAACAADAABAABAAM'. Show all comparisons and state at which positions pattern is found.
keyPoints: Build LPS for 'AAB': [0,1,0]
Preprocessing complete in O(3) time
Searching: traverse text with pattern indices
Show detailed comparison at each step
Pattern found at positions: 0 (AAB), 9 (AAB), 12 (AAB)
Demonstrate how LPS helps skip characters after mismatch
Total comparisons shown to be less than naive approach
All three occurrences (including overlapping) are found
